> If this API addition goes through, I'm not sure if it is worth keeping the VIRTUAL_ENV logic: it boils down to having both VIRTUAL_ENV's and jedi's sys.path in one interpreter and this is better accomplished the other way around. I mean, that logic starts with jedi on sys.path and then tries to add venv path afterwards,

Hmm I know what you mean. I personally think that adding `sys_path` as an API is really valuable, because some people really want to play with it. Most of the time this is not the case and we should focus on what happens when it is `None`. This still includes virtual envs. However, I agree totally with the `portable.py` idea. I think Jedi should be started with just the virtualenv as its path. This is what I'm talking about when I talk about Jedi's future async server/client structure. You name a certain virtualenv and Jedi starts a new process that includes that virtualenv. This way, we don't have segfaults and all other crazy stuff...

What do you think? This would however mean that we don't include this pull request, we would rather just add the sys_path logic. And BTW this is not to say that your work is bad. I'm really thankful for it. It taught me a lot! :)

~ Dave

Well, it's a little trickier than that. The records for the relevant DNS zone is requested (which is why that is indicated in args part of the docstring), but what is returned is the records for the managed zone that corresponds to that DNS zone (which is why I left it in this part of the docstring).  Because gordon-gcp currently puts all records in the same DNS zone, this is not an important distinction, *but* if any user of the client were to pass in reverse records it is significant, since the scheme we use for now has a many to one relationship of DNS zone[s] -> managed zone because we reduce to the two most significant octets in reverse zones.  For example `get_records_for_zone('30.20.10.in-addr.arpa')` returns records from that `30.20.10.in-addr.arpa`, but also might return records from `31.20.10.in-addr.arpa`.

It is encapsulation, one of the 3 benefits of OO.

TL;DR.

The pattern of such test generator always include a generator and one (or more) helper. The helper(s) is tightly coupled with the generator, and is unlikely to be reused by others. The class encapsulation makes it explicit that these 2 guys always sit together. The conceptual benefit may not be obvious here but, should this file evolve to contain more test cases in future, I believe cases grouped by several classes would look better than [a bunch of flattened functions like this](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/tests/integration/test_smoke.py#L181-L249).

I got the O(N) L2 error search to work (with one slight alteration to optimize the integral instead of the sum).  This is definitely a big improvement in speed -- bringing the amount of time needed to thin the LSST bandpasses down from 80s to 2s on my laptop.  The results in accuracy are more complicated.  For a reminder, here's the results of running Jim's script for the slow (but optimal) O(N^2) split point search:

![color_comp](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/14802423/86677c34-0b05-11e6-8aba-57f226d979e6.png)

And then here's the result for the O(N) not-quite-optimal split point search:

![default_color_comp](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/14802432/a40f50a4-0b05-11e6-8c20-6707a4376fe4.png)

The approximate search doesn't do quite as good of a job in optimizing \int abs(f(x) - approx(x)).  This is despite the fact that the stopping criterion is the same for both algorithms.  I dug a little deeper and looked at the specific combinations of bandpass and SED that were being problematic in the u-band.  In the following figure, the blue shaded region is the u-band throughput, the red shaded region is one of the problematic SEDs, and the green&blue lines are the original and thinned products of these (all normalized to unit maximum).  The red line is the difference between the thinned and unthinned product.

![u-band](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3650485/14802500/152e47f4-0b06-11e6-9fc0-413d4a0fb85c.png)

The problematic wavelengths seem to all be due to light leaks.  I can't quite wrap my head around it, but I think this might be something like the L2 search favoring fixing a narrow but more- discrepant region vs a wide but less-discrepant region compared to the L-inf search.

In the end, I think maybe one more keyword with a default, something like `exact_search=False`, may be appropriate, together with a warning in the documentation that the thinning target is only accurate for photometry with respect to a flat (in photons/unit-wavelength) SED.

@fxdgear @HonzaKral is right. If you have ssl_context you do not need cert_file and key_file. But it is depend on the python version.
If someone using python 2.7.8 ( ssl_context is introduced in 2.7.9!), can not use ssl_context. So, cert_file and key_file is used.
For example:
python 2.7.8:
```python
import ssl 
ssl.wrap_socket(socket, keyfile, certfile, ca_certs, cert_reqs=ssl.CERT_REQUIRED)
```
python 2.7.9:
```python
import ssl
context = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1)
#load the certificates
....
context.wrap_socket(socket, serverside=False)
```
I propose to remove cert_file and key_file, because it is inconsistent and up to the developer to create the ssl_context. We are moved to python 2.7.13 this solution is okay for me. But when I opened #471 is was not the case!

I mean that Observers can be out of date a bit comparing to other Validators and Observers. In other words, observers may be at a different state at a given time => Observers may have different pool states.
So, a client needs to be able to work with different Observers in their current state => clients may face different pool state.
If clients send query requests to different observers, they may face different state of the pools (needed to verify BLS multisig), so we need to have Patricia merkle state Trie for clients.

* release signing is restricted to [these repos](https://github.com/escapewindow/scriptworker/blob/8e9c08381e049fed24a111da1819f68e3f5e1c78/scriptworker/constants.py#L207-L214)
* nightly signing is restricted to [this superset of repos](https://github.com/escapewindow/scriptworker/blob/8e9c08381e049fed24a111da1819f68e3f5e1c78/scriptworker/constants.py#L220-L230)

I am afraid that this is not enough to determine if we are syncing.

`eth_syncing` will be returning `false` even if we are not synced but if the syncing process has not yet started. And since some times the syncing process may take even minutes to start then this is not the best way to detect if we are synced.

A better way would be to use some oracle of the latest block and compare if we are close to it, let's say 5 blocks from it.

The simplest oracle I can imagine would be etherscan, or we could have our own dedicated server. For etherscan that would be the following API call:

`https://ropsten.etherscan.io/api?module=proxy&action=eth_blockNumber`
